title: Integer quantum Hall effect
abstract: Integer quantum Hall effect, which is the Hall effect quantized into integer times $e^2/h$ ($e$: elementary charge, $h$: Planck's constant) observed first in two-dimensional electron gases in strong magnetic fields, is reviewed from both theoretical and experimental standpoints. Basic physics underlying the phenomenon is explained. Specifically in this new edition we have a fresh look at how the quantum Hall effect is captured in a perspective of topological systems, since, while the quantum Hall effect is historically the first realization of the topological systems, the field has been delved into a much wider realm of physics of topological systems. We also mention diverse advances such as the quantum Hall effect (QHE) in various materials and contexts that include graphene, oxides and narrow-gap semiconductors, a relation with the fractional quantum Hall effect, and the quantum Hall effect as the resistance standard and further roles in the new SI system. We also expound the Floquet topological insulator (a light-matter coupled system) as a new paradigm in nonequilibrium topological systems, where an anomalous quantum Hall effect in zero magnetic field is realized as theoretically predicted to occur in graphene illuminated by a circularly-polarized laser and experimentally verified recently.
summary: This comprehensive review details the Integer Quantum Hall Effect, establishing its foundation in topological physics. It then advances the concept by analyzing its realization in disordered systems and, most significantly, extends it to non-equilibrium Floquet topological insulators driven by light. This shows how topological quantization can emerge from time-dependent quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The paper reviews the Integer Quantum Hall Effect (IQHE), explaining its underlying physics from theoretical and experimental standpoints, and placing it within the broader context of topological systems.
main_result: The IQHE is fundamentally linked to topological invariants, and the concept has been extended to non-equilibrium systems like Floquet topological insulators, realizing anomalous QHE without a static magnetic field.
method: The analysis employs theoretical tools ranging from effective-mass approximations and Kubo formalism to advanced concepts like Floquet theory and Berry's curvature integration.
model_or_system: The primary systems studied are two-dimensional electron gases (2DEGs) in strong magnetic fields, realized in semiconductor heterostructures, and graphene subjected to time-periodic driving fields (Floquet systems).
key_observables: Quantized Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), Landau levels, topological invariants (Chern number), and quasi-energies.
important_parameters:
Magnetic field strength ($B$), filling factor ($
u$), magnetic length ($\ell$), and laser frequency ($\Omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the clean limit, the lowest subband approximation, and approximations like the self-consistent Born approximation or high-frequency expansions for Floquet systems.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the topological classification of quantum states, the energy spectra (Landau levels and Floquet subbands), and the characteristic plateaus in Hall and longitudinal conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$ vs. gate voltage).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the classical and quantum description of the IQHE in static fields, through the inclusion of disorder (localization theory), and culminates in the extension of QHE concepts to time-periodically driven (Floquet) systems.
why_interesting: The extension to Floquet systems provides a direct link between quantum optics/AMO physics (laser driving) and fundamental condensed matter phenomena (topological quantization), making it highly relevant for open quantum systems research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Another representation of the table of topological systems, after A.P. Schnyder, S. Ryu, A. Furusaki, and A.W.W. Ludwig, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125 (2008); S. Ryu et al, New J. Phys. 12, 065010 (2010). Purple bands highlight TI (topological insulator) and SC (superconductor) series, orange squares (Z2, Z2, Z) triplets, while red inscriptions denote examples of the quantum states as in the previous figure.
Figure 3: Topological boundary states for various topological states on the table of topological systems. TR stands for the time-reversal symmetry.

Figure 4: Structures of MOSFET (a) and semiconductor heterostructure (b). In each frame, the upper panel shows the sample structure, while the lower panel the electronic band struc- ture around the interface. Typical wavefunctions are plotted in black against the direction perpecdicular to the interface.
Figure 5: Classical orbits for a charged particle in a magnetic field (∥z) in an applied electric field (∥x) for a clean system (a) and in a disordered system with scatterers (crosses) (b).

Figure 6: (a) Hall-bar sample geometry for measuring the QHE. (b) Top views of a Hall bar and a Corbino sample, with source (S) and drain (D) electrodes. (c) A birds-eye view of the equipotential lines in the QHE condition.
